The general principles for soft tissue surgery in guinea 
pigs are the same as for other companion mammals. How-
ever, some of these principles are particularly important 
(a)
to reduce postsurgical mortality in this species: minimizing 
surgical time and blood loss will indirectly contribute to 
maintaining body temperature, and gentle handling of the 
abdominal viscera will help to prevent adhesion formation 
and gastrointestinal stasis.
Equipment
The size of surgical instruments must be appropriate for 
the animal’s small size. As a result, the most suitable surgical 
instruments are human paediatric, vascular or ophthalmic 
instruments. A basic surgical kit should consist of a pair of 
atraumatic Adson forceps, straight and curved Hartman 
mosquito forceps, iris or tenotomy scissors, and a small 
needle holder. Surgical ring retractors (Figure 12.1) or even 
Barraquer blepharostats of different sizes can be useful.
The use of transparent or semi-transparent plastic 
drapes allows easier observation and monitoring of the 
patient during the surgical procedure. Plastic drapes also 
help to maintain body heat and keep the patient dry during 
irrigation and flushing procedures. To minimize skin  (b)
trauma, plastic drapes can be held in place with smaller, 
less traumatic clamps or stuck to the patient’s skin with

Chapter 12 • Soft tissue surgery
Early ingestion of food should be encouraged by offer-
ing the guinea pig’s favourite items, especially fresh greens 
that will contribute to gastrointestinal motility. In patients 
that show hyporexia or anorexia, assisted feeding might 
be necessary (see Chapter 8). Production of faeces during 
the first 24 hours after surgery is expected; if this does 
not occur, efforts should be made to rule out hypomotility. 
Many veterinary surgeons (veterinarians) routinely admin-
ister prokinetic drugs after surgery to ensure postoperative 
gastrointestinal motility.
Cutaneous masses
Common integumentary surgical procedures include repair 
of skin trauma, removal of epithelial or subcutaneous 
masses, and surgical excision of abscesses. See Chapter 
14 for further information about skin disease.
Trichofolliculomas are benign neoplasms of the hair

Abscesses can be lanced with a scalpel blade to allow 
drainage of the purulent material. Lancing should be fol-
lowed by irrigation of the abscess cavity with warm saline, 
dilute chlorhexidine or povidone–iodine and then the inci-
sion should be left open to heal by second intention. 
However, recurrence is common with this approach as the 
abscess capsule is not completely excised. If it is left in 
place, the capsule serves as a nidus of infection that will 
lead to recurrence of the abscess. For this reason, com-
plete surgical excision using an elliptical incision around 
the abscess and treating it as though it were a soft tissue 
mass, to ensure that all the capsule is eliminated, is pre-
ferred to lancing and draining.
Cutaneous neoplasia
Skin neoplasia represents the second most commonly 
reported group of neoplasms in guinea pigs. Most skin 
neoplasms are benign and cystic; they include trichoepi-
thelioma, trichofolliculoma (Figure 12.3) and sebaceous 
adenoma. Excision of any skin mass is recommended (see 
Chapter 14) (Mullen, 2000).
Cytological examination of a sample obtained by fine-
needle aspiration might help to determine the type of 
neoplasia and whether wide surgical margins are 
required. When planning the excision of a neoplastic 
mass, the shape and tension of the resulting wound 
should be assessed and planned beforehand in case a 
skin flap is needed. A boar with a tumour of the right mammary gland

Potential complications are abscessation secondary to 
infection or a foreign body reaction (e.g. to suture material) 
at the surgical site and herniation of abdominal organs 
(intestines, seminal vesicles) through the inguinal canal. 
Haemorrhage from the testicular vessels might go unde-
tected because the vascular pedicle retracts into the 
abdomen once it has been detached from the testicle. 
Subcutaneous haematoma or bleeding is secondary to 
haemorrhage from vessels in the skin or tunica vaginalis.
Ovariectomy and ovariohysterectomy
Ovariectomy is faster and easier to perform and causes 
less morbidity than ovariohysterectomy, but is not advised 
in older guinea pigs, especially if ovarian cysts are present, 
because these animals may already have uterine disease

Total ear canal ablation and lateral bulla 
osteotomy
Indications for total ear canal ablation and lateral bulla 
osteotomy (TECALBO) are otitis media and/or otitis 
interna, conditions for which medical treatment is unfor-
tunately often ineffective. The goal of a TECALBO is to 
remove the pus present in the tympanic bulla, as well as 
the infected bone if osteomyelitis is present. As the ear 
canal of the guinea pig is very short and horizontal, it is 
technically not possible to perform a partial ear canal 
ablation like that described in rabbits (Wells et al., 1986; 
Volait and Pignon, 2015).
The animal is placed in lateral recumbency and the 
ear is shaved and surgically prepared. Two parallel cuta-

OPERATIVE TECHNIQUE 12.2 
Flank ovariohysterectomy
Patient preparation
The right flank is clipped and prepared only in the craniodorsal area, whereas the left flank is clipped and prepared 
from the lateral processes of the lumbar vertebrae to the ventral abdomen, and from the last rib to the hindlimb.
Patient positioning
The patient is positioned in left lateral recumbency, with the right flank facing the surgeon. If the patient is placed on 
an inclined surface (e.g. an operating table that can be tilted laterally, or on top of a raised block covered with a pad) 
in such a way that the ventral part of the body is lower than the dorsal side, the abdominal viscera will move ventrally, 
away from the ovaries and uterus.
